item.title: Cheap Drones Remain Wild Card in Iran war
item.description: Stopping Iran’s production of drones is critical to opening the Strait of Hormuz and halting its attacks on Gulf nations. But can it be done?

item.title: FirstFT: Markets rally on hopes for a breakthrough in the Middle East
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: Democrat wins Mar-a-Lago state seat and blue-chip companies speed up debt-raising plans

item.title: Dozens of bodies, mostly infants, discovered in Kenya mass grave
item.description: Some of the 32 bodies, including 25 children, are believed to have come from local hospitals and mortuaries.

item.title: Pentagon Orders 2,000 Airborne Troops to Middle East
item.description: The deployment could give President Trump more military options after a 15-point proposal, delivered via Pakistan, was sent to the Iranians, according to two officials briefed on the diplomacy.

item.title: Four Takeaways From Denmark’s Election
item.description: A desire for change and a fractured political landscape left Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen ahead, but with a tough road to securing a new term.

item.title: Ukraine Finally Got Battlefield Momentum. Now Comes a Russian Offensive.
item.description: Moscow’s forces are intensifying their attacks in southern Ukraine after Kyiv made rare gains along the front.

item.title: For Australia’s Farmers, Fuel Crisis Comes at ‘Worst Possible Time’
item.description: Their troubles could cascade across Asia because they export a majority of their products.

item.title: Two Men Arrested in Attack on Jewish Charity Ambulances in London
item.description: The police said the men, aged 45 and 47, are accused of arson with intent to endanger life stemming from the arson attack on Monday in Golders Green.

item.title: London police arrest two men over ambulance arson attacks
item.description: Met hails significant breakthrough in investigation into antisemitic attack

item.title: Reeves is right to resist universal help on energy bills
item.description: UK’s fiscal constraints leave government reliant on blunter, supplier-side support

item.title: Inside Trump’s Secret Deal to Deport Migrants to Cameroon
item.description: In Cameroon, the Trump administration found a partner it could pressure into accepting covertly deported migrants.

item.title: An American in Russia Is Linked to Neo-Nazi Terror Cells Across Europe
item.description: F.B.I. agents thought they had weakened an online hate group known as the Base. A string of European terrorism cases indicates it has resurged.

item.title: Marcos promises 'flow of oil' as Philippines declares energy emergency
item.description: The President says the country will procure one million barrels of oil to add to the current stock.

item.title: Lagarde says ECB ready to raise interest rates ‘at any meeting’
item.description: Central bank chief says policymakers need time to assess ‘nature, size and persistence’ of inflation surge from Iran war

item.title: Graffiti conviction for Australian woman who stuck googly eyes on sculpture
item.description: The woman had taken MDMA and three litres of vodka when she vandalised the piece nicknamed Blue Blob.

item.title: Danish PM fails to secure majority in party's weakest election showing since 1903
item.description: The party, which has been in power since 2019, gained the most votes but faces tough talks on forming a new government.

item.title: Stale February inflation data will not have much bearing on BoE
item.description: Effects of Iran war will only start to show from March

item.title: UK inflation held steady at 3% in February before energy shock
item.description: Figure comes as Middle East war has triggered a surge in oil and gas prices

item.title: What Americans moving to the UK need to know about tax
item.description: To make sure you avoid the traps, time your arrival and restructure your trusts

item.title: How is the Philippines reacting to its energy emergency?
item.description: BBC News Asia Business Correspondent Suranjana Tewari reports from a petrol station in Manila.

item.title: Indian man whose life support was removed after court go-ahead dies
item.description: Harish Rana, 31, had been in a comatose state since 2013 after he fell from a fourth-floor balcony.

item.title: What’s moving long-term interest rates?
item.description: Plus, US bank regulations

item.title: Journalists at Australia's national broadcaster strike for first time in 20 years
item.description: Staff have walked out for 24 hours over a pay rise offer that was below inflation.

item.title: Has banning phones improved performance at Dutch schools?
item.description: Two years ago the Netherlands banned phones in schools. Now the government wants to go further, pushing to restrict social media for under‑16s.

item.title: Germany’s finance minister faces ‘Schröder moment’ amid regional election fallout
item.description: Also in this newsletter: the fight over hosting the EU’s customs agency

item.title: 'Major player' in alleged mistaken identity kidnap of Sydney grandfather charged
item.description: The 23-year-old is the third man to be charged in connection with the death of Chris Baghsarian.

item.title: Bank of England not as hawkish as market thinks
item.description: Read the March forecast from the FT’s Monetary Policy Radar team

item.title: Oil slides below $100 after US sends Iran plan to end conflict
item.description: Washington sent 15-point proposal to Tehran via Pakistan in recent days

item.title: We must not underestimate the peril for democracy
item.description: Donald Trump’s America is a world leader in democratic decline

item.title: A suitor eyes Jefferies
item.description: Japan’s Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group is working on plans for a possible takeover of the Wall Street investment bank

item.title: Nato chief riles Europe by backing Trump’s war in Iran
item.description: European capitals irritated by Mark Rutte’s suggestion they will join US armada to Strait of Hormuz

item.title: Italy’s Meloni looks for gas supplies in Algeria
item.description: Prime minister struggles to find alternatives to Qatari LNG shipments curtailed by Iran war

item.title: Estée Lauder — Puig mix-and-match would be hard to absorb
item.description: Investor disapproval of possible tie-up reflects the challenge of integrating two different family controlled companies

item.title: European airlines hold off jet fuel hedging in Iran war gamble
item.description: Traders expect prices to fall later this year, giving carriers hope they can lock in lower rates by being patient

item.title: Oil and gas companies seize the chance to lobby for Arctic drilling
item.description: Energy security arguments being pushed in response to review of EU policy

item.title: Wellcome’s flagship genetic campus seeks missing transport link
item.description: Facility near Cambridge is undergoing ambitious expansion — but faces logistical challenges

item.title: Companies speed up debt raising plans amid market volatility
item.description: Corporates try to take advantage of market rebounds rather than risk further turbulence or US midterm elections

item.title: Sara Duterte impeachment hearings start as political death match heats up
item.description: The Philippines' VP faces another impeachment round with her 2028 presidential run on the line.

item.title: BTS comeback concert on Netflix draws 18.4m global viewers
item.description: The livestreamed show saw the band perform together for the first time since October 2022.

item.title: The U.S. Military Said It Helped Bomb a Drug Camp in Ecuador. It Was a Dairy Farm.
item.description: The Times visited a village where the United States and Ecuador said they destroyed an armed group’s training camp. Residents said it was actually a dairy farm.

item.title: Social media bans and digital curfews to be trialled on UK teenagers
item.description: The government will interview the young people and their parents before and after they try the limits to assess their impact.

item.title: Russia launches 948 drones at Ukraine in largest attack over 24-hour period
item.description: Cities across Ukraine were hit, with local officials reporting at least eight people killed and dozens injured.

item.title: Death toll from Colombian military plane crash rises to 69 as search ends
item.description: The Hercules military transport plane crashed minutes after take-off near Colombia's southern border with Peru.

item.title: Investigators Seek Answers in Attacks on Jewish Sites in Europe
item.description: Attacks on schools and property in several countries have Jewish communities on edge, amid suspicions that Iran is behind the violence.

item.title: Salah to leave Liverpool at end of season
item.description: The Egypt forward says Liverpool fans "gave me the best time of my life" and "stood by me in the toughest times".

item.title: Reform candidate suspended over Jewish group comments
item.description: Chris Parry was suspended "pending investigation" after a series of social media posts.

item.title: Costa Rica Agrees to Take Migrants Deported by the Trump Administration
item.description: The agreement is part of President Trump’s efforts to find governments willing to accept people who have been detained in the United States.

item.title: China’s government both drives and constrains the rise of AI
item.description: Officials want to lead the world in the new technology but are still wary of disruption

item.title: Italy’s Meloni Seemed Unbeatable. A Referendum Defeat Has Dented Her Aura.
item.description: Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni had led one of Italy’s most stable postwar governments. Now she’s under pressure after failing to convince Italians to back a judicial overhaul.

item.title: Take Shorter Showers: South Korea Kicks Off an Energy-Saving Drive
item.description: President Lee Jae Myung called on the public to cooperate, likening the energy supply disruption caused by the Iran war to the Asian financial crisis and the pandemic.

item.title: Is there an election in my area?
item.description: Elections are taking place across the country in May. Use our postcode lookup to find out if there are elections where you live.

item.title: 'Truly magical woman': Tributes to Married at First Sight's Mel Schilling
item.description: The TV dating coach is remembered as "a radiant, shining light" following her death at the age of 54.

item.title: Why the 7 May elections matter
item.description: BBC's Chris Mason explains what's at stake in the upcoming elections across the UK.

item.title: Everyday life in Asia is being upended by Iran war fuel crisis
item.description: Asia relies heavily on oil and gas from the Gulf, and shortages and higher prices are starting to bite.

item.title: Israel says it will take control of large buffer zone in southern Lebanon
item.description: Thousands of displaced Lebanese residents will not be allowed to return home until northern Israel is safe, Israel's defence minister says.

item.title: Sturgeon denies 'obsession' with gender reforms when Scotland's first minister
item.description: The ex-SNP leader, who is stepping down from Holyrood this week, has defended her support for the trans community.

item.title: Marco Rubio testifies in former housemate's Maduro lobbying trial
item.description: Rubio's former housemate is charged with illegally trying to influence the first Trump administration on behalf of the Venezuelan government.

item.title: Ghana and EU sign landmark defence deal to combat militant Islamist threat
item.description: Ghana is the first African country to make such a deal with the EU, the Ghanaian vice-president says.

item.title: Afghanistan Frees American Dennis Coyle from Detention
item.description: Dennis Walter Coyle, a researcher from Colorado, had been held since last year by the Taliban government.

item.title: Woman sentenced over threats to kill Angela Rayner
item.description: Elizabeth Harker, 63, from Luton, is given a community order after admitting leaving the messages.

item.title: US detainee Dennis Coyle released by Taliban after more than a year
item.description: Coyle, who was working as a language researcher, according to his family, was arrested in January 2025.

item.title: Reeves plans energy bill help for those 'who need it most'
item.description: The US-Israel war with Iran is having an impact on costs domestically, with oil and gas prices soaring.

item.title: Trump Sours on Keir Starmer Over UK’s Decision Not to Join Attacks on Iran
item.description: President Trump once called Prime Minister Keir Starmer a friend. But Britain’s decision not to join the attacks on Iran has led to merciless mocking by the president.

item.title: Heat pumps for all new homes and plug-in solar in green tech drive
item.description: Solar panels that can be plugged in at home could be available to buy in supermarkets in the coming months.

item.title: Government Cuts Gut the Memory of Argentina’s Dirty War
item.description: Fifty years after the military dictatorship, Argentina’s government is defunding human rights groups and promoting a revisionist account of the junta’s crimes.

item.title: Botswana prepares to take an even bigger gamble on diamonds
item.description: The African country wants to own more of De Beers, the firm that helped it to escape poverty

item.title: Irish government cuts excise duty on diesel and petrol
item.description: Duty on diesel will be reduced by 20 cent and petrol will be cut by 15 cent from midnight on Wednesday.

item.title: Lib Dems fix communities, says Davey at election campaign launch
item.description: The Lib Dem leader highlights policies on the NHS and energy bills ahead of council polls in England.

item.title: How Pakistan Is Trying to Reshape Its Image Abroad
item.description: New, friendly media operations and expanded state-run television are pushing Pakistan’s message while independent news outlets face repression.

item.title: Australia and EU agree sweeping trade deal in face of global uncertainty
item.description: Australia and the EU sign sweeping trade and security deals after years of negotiations.

item.title: As Denmark Goes to the Polls, Here’s What to Know About the Election and Key Issues
item.description: President Trump’s threats to take Greenland away from Denmark have lifted Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen, who appeared the front-runner as polls opened.

item.title: Elon Musk's Starlink blocked from operating in Namibia
item.description: Namibia's regulator noted that Starlink's subsidiary is not locally owned.

item.title: Ukraine Spent Big to Shield Energy Industry From Drones. Is the Mideast Next?
item.description: With the use of electronic jamming systems and interceptor drones, the Ukrainian national oil and gas company may be a model for others.

item.title: Sinner surpasses Djokovic's Masters 1000 record
item.description: Jannik Sinner surpasses Novak Djokovic's 10-year record for consecutive sets won at ATP Masters 1000 events in his Miami Open win over Corentin Moutet.

item.title: Cuba's mothers-to-be prepare to give birth in a country plunged into darkness
item.description: Two pregnant women tell the BBC's Will Grant of their hopes and fears as their nation is mired in crisis.

item.title: China dials back on fuel price hikes to 'reduce burden' on drivers
item.description: It comes as countries across the region are taking various measures to weather the soaring cost of fuel.

item.title: How North Korea’s Kim Jong-un Is Using the Iran War to Justify His Nuclear Arsenal
item.description: He delivered a lengthy speech at his country’s rubber-stamp Parliament, declaring that his nuclear power will shield his country from American hostility.

item.title: UK must back North Sea oil and gas drilling, says trade body
item.description: The group says the country "urgently" needs to produce its own oil and gas to secure supplies.

item.title: Inside the alleged Russian operation to trigger anti-government protests in Angola
item.description: A group of Russians and Angolans are going on trial in Angola after being accused of fomenting protests, which they deny.

item.title: UK universities flock to India - but will they succeed?
item.description: Nine UK universities are setting up campuses in India, but enrolment is expected to be modest initially.

item.title: Iwobi named on Football Black List alongside Carter and Eze
item.description: England defender Jess Carter, Arsenal attacker Eberechi Eze and Fulham midfielder Alex Iwobi are on the 2025 Football Black List.

item.title: Real talk is nonsense but I may coach again - Klopp
item.description: Former Liverpool manager Jurgen Klopp says speculation he will return to management with Real Madrid this summer is "nonsense".

item.title: Burnham should be allowed to stand as an MP, says Nandy
item.description: The culture secretary questions her party's decision to block the Greater Manchester mayor from standing in a by-election.

item.title: Nigel Farage abandons walkabout due to protest
item.description: The Reform leader visits Felixstowe to launch his party's Suffolk local elections campaign.

item.title: Giorgia Meloni’s big electoral setback in Italy
item.description: Her failed referendum gamble hints at growing dissatisfaction with her government

item.title: Referendum defeat leaves Italy's Meloni looking more vulnerable
item.description: Meloni vowed to press on after losing a vote on constitutional reform many turned into a referendum on her government.

item.title: PM warns against 'false comfort' of thinking Iran war will end quickly
item.description: The PM also tells senior MPs the conflict was "not our war" and there must be a "lawful basis" for any UK involvement.

item.title: Donald Trump’s latest climbdown suggests he wants to end the war
item.description: The divide between Israel and America is widening

item.title: The War Room newsletter: Trump is scrambling for options in Iran
item.description: “To end the war on current terms would look like defeat [for Trump] to most of the world,” writes Shashank Joshi, our defence editor

item.title: A committed pharmacist and a homesick blogger – the Iranian civilians killed in the war
item.description: The toll on civilians is mounting fast as the US-Israeli war on Iran continues.

item.title: Ex-justice secretary to lead review into MP murder
item.description: Sir David Amess's daughter says the family will engage with the independent review in "good faith".

item.title: Slovenia becomes first EU country to introduce fuel rationing
item.description: Until further notice, motorists in Slovenia will be restricted to a maximum purchase of 50 litres of fuel per day.

item.title: Why the number of Islamic schools in Canada is soaring
item.description: Some Muslim parents worry about their children losing touch with their faith

item.title: Campaigners celebrate after new town plans dropped
item.description: Critics of plans to build a new town in Cheshire called it the "wrong proposal in the wrong place".

item.title: Could Iranian missiles reach London and Paris?
item.description: Some experts say Iran may be able to launch missiles to hit London, but they would likely be shot down long before arriving and the risk is low.

item.title: HS2 trains could run slower than planned to save money
item.description: The government is looking at ways to make much-delayed London to Birmingham railway line cheaper to deliver.

item.title: Nigerian heritage key to my success - Itauma
item.description: Moses Itauma, one of heavyweight boxing's brightest prospects, says he found the answers to questions about his identity on a trip to his father's village in Nigeria.

item.title: US' and Iran's options for ending war narrow the longer it goes on
item.description: Iran shows no sign of climbdown, while air power alone cannot achieve the US' main goal.

item.title: Nigerian police arrest 15 after viral videos of alleged sexual assault at festival
item.description: The videos sparked widespread anger, leading to the hashtag #StopRapingWomen to trend on social media.

item.title: Ministers name seven areas where 'new towns' could be built
item.description: Six sites have been deprioritised from the flagship scheme to build a wave of new settlements.

item.title: France's former Socialist Prime Minister Lionel Jospin dies at 88
item.description: Jospin was head of government from 1997 to 2002 and is credited with bringing in the 35-hour working week.

item.title: Plan to scrap most short jail terms comes into effect
item.description: The government hopes it will ease the population crisis in prisons.

item.title: Germany has a shortage of workers - so it's turning to India for help
item.description: The European nation, struggling to find skilled staff, is giving jobs to young people from India.

item.title: France's Socialists hold on to power in major cities in election boost for mainstream
item.description: Paris and Marseille are held by the Socialists in local elections which saw the nationalist right win in Nice and certain towns.

item.title: Israeli settlers target Palestinian villages in occupied West Bank, attacking people and properties
item.description: The violence began after 18-year-old settler Yehuda Sherman was killed after reportedly being hit by a vehicle driven by a Palestinian while on his quad bike.

item.title: National blackout hits Cuba for second time in a week
item.description: Another total failure of its power grid underlines Cuba's hardship under a US-imposed fuel blockade.

item.title: Why did US and Israel attack Iran and how long could the war last?
item.description: Conflict has spread across the Middle East since the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran on 28 February.

item.title: Israel to expand ground and air attacks against Hezbollah in Lebanon
item.description: There are fears in Lebanon that Israel is preparing for a large-scale ground invasion.

item.title: Ukraine’s top drone commander wants to bleed Russia’s army dry
item.description: “Madyar” Brovdi has pioneered unmanned warfare methods NATO is yet to learn

item.title: Donald Trump has four bad options for the war in Iran
item.description: None is guaranteed to end the war or ease the economic crisis it has produced

item.title: No assessment Iran could strike London, UK minister says
item.description: Steve Reed says there is "no specific assessment" Iran is capable of hitting the UK, after Israel claimed it now could.

item.title: Iranian missiles injure 180 in towns near Israeli nuclear site
item.description: Israel is investigating how ballistic missiles got through the country's sophisticated air defences.

item.title: Why Bangalore has India’s best billionaires
item.description: Its philanthropists want to do more than just give money away

item.title: Hillsborough campaigners blame Mahmood for law delay
item.description: The Hillsborough Law has been further delayed and will not be passed in this session of parliament.

item.title: Qatari and Turkish citizens die in military helicopter crash
item.description: Four Qatari military personnel, one Turkish serviceman and two technicians from a Turkish defence company were on board.
